subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CAREW J:

This matter was listed for a five-day trial commencing on 2 February 2026, initially involving both parenting and property matters. On the first day of the trial, the parties consented to the wife’s application for property settlement being dismissed, as there is no net property of the parties or either of them. The parties requested that the order dismissing the application also record by way of notation that each of them would retain any property or liabilities in their respective names.
The parenting proceedings concern two children, X, age 13, and Y, age 12. The children live with their mother and have done so since the parents separated in 2019. X and Y have not spent any time with the father since September 2022 and February 2023 respectively, other than a 15-minute visit by the children to the paternal grandmother in hospital in 2024, at which the father was present.
On the first day of the trial, the solicitor advocate for the mother, Ms Ryder, informed the Court that the mother did not seek a positive finding that the father had strangled or physically assaulted either child, but maintained that the evidence relevant to the allegations would inform my assessment of the risk posed by the father of physical harm to the children. It was anticipated that I would be asked to view in open court several video recordings made by police and the pre-recorded evidence of the child, X, in relation to the strangulation/assault allegations of X for which the father was charged in 2019. The charges were withdrawn in 2021. Obviously, the fact that criminal charges were withdrawn did not resolve the broader issue for this Court, namely, one of assessing the magnitude of any risk posed by the father.
The mother also informed the Court on the first day of trial that she would not seek any findings at all in relation to her allegations that the father had perpetrated family violence against her during the relationship. The trial proceeded on that basis, and counsel for the father and the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) and the solicitor advocate for the mother were invited to amend the list of issues that I would be asked to determine to reflect the concessions made.
On the second day of the trial, the mother made further concessions. The mother’s solicitor advocate informed the Court that the mother no longer contended that the father posed an unacceptable risk of physical harm to the children, and that there was no longer any necessity for the Court to view the video recordings relating to those matters. Cross-examination of the mother continued and given the concessions made by the mother as to the nature of her case against the father, counsel for the father did not challenge the mother in relation to her allegations relating to family violence against the mother, or physical violence or other harm alleged against the father involving the children.
Counsel for the ICL commenced his cross-examination of the mother by interrogating her about her beliefs regarding past violence perpetrated by the father against herself and the children. The mother said that she was convinced that the father had strangled both children. The mother said that notwithstanding her beliefs, she could reconcile that with her proposal that Y should spend unsupervised day time with the father because:
What happened in the past is in the past, and that is okay, and I do believe it happened. Fine. We have to move forward. Parenting courses have been done. There is different elements, as I previously explained.
Those other elements were that the children were older and could voice any issues if they arose, and the fact that there is a female in the house, a reference to the father’s de facto partner, Ms D.
The mother also confirmed that she had made allegations on occasions in the past that the father had put his hands on her throat, choked or strangled her. The mother said that the children saw him do that. The mother’s evidence-in-chief in relation to physical violence perpetrated by the father against her is brief and is set out in her affidavit, paragraphs 157 to 160, in which she says:
There was an occasion when [the father] pushed me to the ground, pulled on me and spun me around to get to the door.
There was another occasion when he put his hands around my neck as if to try and strangle me.
On another occasion he grabbed a scarf that I was wearing and tried to drag me toward the door.
160. [The father] would lash out at me when he was angry at me.
The mother does not allege in her sworn evidence that the father did strangle her, nor does she allege that the children were present for the incident described in her affidavit.
The mother’s evidence-in-chief does not address the allegations of the father’s alleged strangling of X, other than to state that he was charged, and that charges were not pursued because of insufficient evidence. However, the father deposes in his affidavit of evidence-in-chief that he, the mother and the children were travelling in their car in or around June 2019 when X:
131. ...randomly and without prompting, said that I choked her...
The father said he had not done so and was confused about why the child had said this. The father deposed that the mother said she believed X. It is entirely unclear why the mother is “convinced” or said she was convinced that the father choked both children in the past.
I was not taken to any evidence that would explain the mother’s evidence in response to questions by counsel for the ICL. However, during her cross-examination by counsel for the father, she referred to having been told something by her aunt, who was present in Court throughout the trial, suggesting that X had told her that the father had choked her. The mother does not refer to that in her evidence-in-chief and could provide no explanation as to why her aunt was not a witness in her case, if, indeed, she had been told this. The father has his own theory about the allegations, but does not seek any finding in relation thereto, as the parties have now reached agreement about a parenting order that they both contend is in their children’s best interests.
If I make an order in terms of the agreement, the children will live with the father and not spend any time with the mother for three months; thereafter, the children will spend six supervised fortnightly visits with the mother for two hours at a contact centre; and thereafter, the children will spend time with the mother on alternate weekends and half-holidays. The father will have sole parental responsibility and decision-making in relation to major long-term issues, and the mother will be required to attend upon her psychologist, Mr E throughout 2026 and into 2027, and to have Mr E confirm in writing to the father that she continues to do so, at least for so long as he considers that necessary.
When the father was spending supervised time with the children between 18 June 2022 and about 5 November 2022, the visits were observed to be very positive, with both children engaging affectionately and easily with the father, although on a few occasions X was reticent initially, and on a few other occasions she did not attend. The father spent unsupervised time with Y thereafter until about February 2023. Thereafter, the mother did not facilitate time, notwithstanding a consent order that she do so.
The children disclosed during the supervised visits a number of complaints about the person whom the children and mother were living with from August 2019 until June 2025, Mr C. The mother’s failure to facilitate the children’s time with the father coincided with her becoming aware that the children had made complaints about Mr C during the supervised visits.
The mother, during her cross-examination, demonstrated the limited efforts she has made to meet her obligations to facilitate the children’s time with the father in accordance with the order, including via FaceTime. Sadly, the children have not spent any time with the father for a lengthy period. Some attempts were made at family therapy, but as Mr F, the Family Report writer opined, the mother needed to give the children permission to have a relationship with the father, and on hearing her evidence over two days, it seems to me that she consistently failed to do so.
The parents have reached agreement on final orders, and for that they should be commended. Mr Poed, who took over as the solicitor advocate for the mother, indicated that his client had realised that her “belief patterns” were mistaken and unhelpful, and he stressed that his client believes that the order to which she is now agreeing is in the best interests of the children.
The ICL joins in recommending the proposed order, and does so with the knowledge and understanding of a great deal of other evidence that is not before me, consisting of more than 1000 pages of subpoenaed material, which she has reviewed, and also having viewed all of the police recorded interviews with the children and the pre-recorded evidence of X. The ICL has satisfied herself and supports a submission made by her counsel that had all of that evidence been before the Court, I would comfortably have come to the conclusion that the father does not present an unacceptable risk of harm to the children, notwithstanding a conclusion reached by the Department of Families, Seniors, Disability Services and Child Safety (as it is now named) some years ago substantiating an allegation of physical harm to the children from the father. The Department, of course, would not have had the extent of evidence that the ICL had access to, nor, indeed, the benefit of hearing cross-examination of the mother over a day and a half, and the evidence that was relied upon by each party in these proceedings.
Given the mother’s concessions about Mr C’s behaviour towards the children, involving him yelling and swearing at them and, at least on one occasion, physically pushing one of the children, he is not a person who should come into contact with the children, and the proposed injunction restraining the mother from bringing the children into contact with him seems entirely appropriate.
I am persuaded that the order proposed by the parents and recommended by the ICL is in the children’s best interests.
